title: Authors and Citation
text: Fatih Kızılaslan. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Kızılaslan F (2026). SSReliabilityClaytonMWD: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula. R package version 0.1.0, https://github.com/fatihki/SSReliabilityClaytonMWD.
code: @Manual{,   title = {SSReliabilityClaytonMWD: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula},   author = {Fatih Kızılaslan},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://github.com/fatihki/SSReliabilityClaytonMWD}, }

title: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula
text: R package, SSReliabilityClaytonMWD, implements dependent stress–strength reliability (SSR) model Modified Weibull marginals Clayton copula dependence, based study Kızılaslan (2026). package includes: Density, distribution, quantile, random generation functions MWD Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) Least Squares Estimation (LSE) Weighted Least Squares Estimation (WLSE) Maximum Product Spacings (MPS) Dependent SSR modeling via Clayton copula Estimation SSR via MLE, LSE, WLSE, MPS Asymptotic confidence intervals (ACI) Bootstrap confidence intervals (BCIs)

title: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula
text: can install development version GitHub:
code: # install.packages("remotes") # remotes::install_github("fatihki/SSReliabilityClaytonMWD") library(SSReliabilityClaytonMWD)

title: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula
text: modified Weibull distribution (MWD), introduced Lai et al. (2003), widely used reliability survival analysis. random variable XX said follow modified Weibull distribution cumulative distribution function (cdf) F(x)F(x) probability density function (pdf) f(x)f(x) given F(x)=1−exp(−axbeλx),     F(x) = 1- \exp \big( -x^b e^{\lambda x} \big), f(x)=(b+λx)xb−1eλxexp(−axbeλx),    f(x) = (b + \lambda x) x^{b-1} e^{\lambda x} \exp \big( -x^b e^{\lambda x} \big) ,  x>0x>0, >0a>0 scale parameter, b≥0b \ge 0 shape parameter, λ≥0\lambda \ge 0 acceleration flexibility parameter controls quickly hazard grows time. λ=0\lambda=0, reduces two-parameter Weibull distribution F(x)=1−exp(−axb)F(x) = 1- \exp(-x^b). b=0b=0, reduces type extreme-value (log-gamma) distribution F(x)=1−exp(−aeλx)F(x) = 1- \exp(- e^{\lambda x} ).

title: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula
code: # Parameters a1 <- 5; b1 <- 0.75; lambda1 <- 0.5 a2 <- 5; b2 <- 1.5; lambda2 <- 0.5  x <- seq(1e-02, 3, length.out = 1000)  # First curve plot(x, dMweibull(x, a1, b1, lambda1),      type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = 1,      xlab = "x", ylab = "pdf",      main = "Modified Weibull Probability Density Function")  # Second curve lines(x, dMweibull(x, a2, b2, lambda2),       lwd = 2, lty = 2) # Legend legend("topright",        legend = c(expression(b1==1.5), expression(b2==0.75)),        lty = c(1, 2),        lwd = 2,  bty = "n")

title: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula
code: plot(x, pMweibull(x, a1, b1, lambda1), type = "l", lwd = 2,  lty = 1,      xlab = "x", ylab = "cdf",      main = "Modified Weibull Cumulative Distribution Function") # Second curve lines(x, pMweibull(x, a2, b2, lambda2),       lwd = 2, lty = 2) # Legend legend("topright",        legend = c(expression(b1==1.5), expression(b2==0.75)),        lty = c(1, 2),        lwd = 2,  bty = "n")

title: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula
code: plot(x, hMweibull(x, a1, b1, lambda1), type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = 1,      xlab = "x", ylab = "hazard",  ylim = c(0,40),      main = "Modified Weibull Hazard Function") # Second curve lines(x, hMweibull(x, a2, b2, lambda2),       lwd = 2, lty = 2) # Legend legend("topleft",        legend = c(expression(b1==1.5), expression(b2==0.75)),        lty = c(1, 2),        lwd = 2,  bty = "n")

title: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula
code: a <- 0.75; b <- 1.25; lambda <- 0.6 set.seed(123) X <- rMweibull(100, a, b, lambda) hist(X, breaks = 20, main = "Simulated MWD Data", xlab = "X")

title: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula
text: Estimation MWD parameters using MLE, LSE, WLSE MPS methods. Similarly, two-parameter Weibull distribution can also fitted data bySSReliabilityClaytonMWD::fitWD.
code: # Fit Modified Weibull Distribution set.seed(123) init <- runif(3) fit.mle <- fitMWD( data = X, est.method = "mle", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,   lower = c(1e-05, 1e-05, 1e-05), upper = c(Inf, Inf, Inf)) fit.lse <- fitMWD( data = X, est.method = "lse", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,   lower = c(1e-05, 1e-05, 1e-05), upper = c(Inf, Inf, Inf)) fit.wlse <- fitMWD( data = X, est.method = "wlse", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,   lower = c(1e-05, 1e-05, 1e-05), upper = c(Inf, Inf, Inf)) fit.mps <- fitMWD( data = X, est.method = "mps", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,   lower = c(1e-05, 1e-05, 1e-05), upper = c(Inf, Inf, Inf)) # estimates results

title: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula
text: stress–strength reliability R=P(X>Y)R = P(X > Y) dependent framework, stress XX strength YY variables follow MWD dependence modeled via Clayton copula. joint distribution function two-dimensional Clayton copula, along joint probability density function, given Cθ(u,v)=(u−θ+v−θ−1)−1/θ, C_{\theta}(u,v) = (u^{-\theta} + v^{-\theta} - 1)^{-1/\theta}, cθ(u,v)=(θ+1)u−(θ+1)v−(θ+1)(u−θ+v−θ−1)−(1θ+2), c_{\theta}(u,v) = (\theta +1)  u^{-(\theta + 1)} v^{-(\theta + 1)} \big( u^{-\theta} + v^{-\theta}-1 \big)^{-\left (\frac{1}{\theta} + 2 \right)},  θ>0\theta >0. X∼MWD(a1,b1,λ1)X \sim MWD(a_1,b_1,\lambda_1), Y∼MWD(a2,b2,λ2)Y \sim MWD(a_2,b_2,\lambda_2) two-dimensional Clayton copula , RR becomes R=∫0∞FX(x)−(θ+1)(FX(x)−θ+GY(x)−θ−1)−(1θ+1)fX(x)dx=∫01t−(θ+1)(t−θ+GY(FX−1(t))−θ−1)−(1θ+1)dt,   R = \int_{0}^{\infty}  F_X(x)^{-(\theta + 1)} \big(F_X(x)^{-\theta} + G_Y(x)^{-\theta}-1 \big) ^{-\left (\frac{1}{\theta}+1 \right)} f_X(x) \mathrm{d}x     = \int_{0}^{1} t^{-(\theta +1)} \big( t^{-\theta} + G_Y(F_{X}^{-1}(t))^{-\theta} -1 \big)^ {-\left(\frac{1}{\theta}+1 \right) } \mathrm{d}t,  FX(x)≡FX(x;a1,b1,λ1)=1−exp(−a1xb1eλ1x)F_X(x) \equiv F_X(x;a_1,b_1,\lambda_1)  = 1- \exp(-a_1 x^{b_1} e^{\lambda_1 x}) GY(y)≡GY(y;a2,b2,λ2)=1−exp(−a2yb2eλ2y)G_Y(y) \equiv G_Y(y;a_2,b_2,\lambda_2) = 1- \exp(-a_2 y^{b_2} e^{\lambda_2 y}).

what: Real data analysis: Istanbul’s dams data
title: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula
text: applicability proposed SSR model illustrated using monthly occupancy data Istanbul’s two largest dams, Clayton copula capturing dependence structure. data obtained open data platform Istanbul Metropolitan Municipality https://data.ibb.gov.tr/en/. dataset consists daily occupancy rates Istanbul’s dams, retrieved March 2026 data-website. Additionally, daily occupancy rates data Istanbul’s dams available package AllDams. study, focus two largest dams: Omerli (Anatolian side) Terkos (European side). data span period late October 20002000 mid-February 20242024.reduce variability focus seasonal effects, compute monthly average occupancy rates period September–December year. results total 95 paired observations variables. used Omerli Terkos dams data available package OmerliDam TerkosDam. stress–strength framework, Terkos dam treated strength variable XX, Omerli dam considered stress variable YY. formulation, SSR R=P(X>Y)R=P(X>Y) represents probability occupancy level Terkos dam (European side) exceeds Omerli dam (Anatolian side). probability quantifies likelihood local water availability European side sufficient relative corresponding level Anatolian side. practical terms, Terkos level falls Omerli, additional water transfer European side may required. higher value RR indicates favorable scenario, implying reduced need water transfer lower operational costs, whereas lower values RR suggest increased reliance inter-regional water transfer , consequently, higher system costs risks. details can found Kızılaslan (2026).

title: Fitting Stress-Strength Reliability Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula
text: Lai, C. D., Xie, M., & Murthy, D. N. P. (2003). modified Weibull distribution. IEEE Transactions Reliability, 52(1), 33–37. doi.org/10.1109/TR.2002.805788 Kızılaslan, Fatih. (2026). Reliability estimation dependent stress–strength model Clayton copula modified Weibull margins.arXiv:26????

title: All Istanbul Dams Data — AllDams
text: Daily data 10 dams Istanbul, Türkiye. dataset consists daily occupancy rates Istanbul’s dams, retrieved March 2026 https://data.ibb.gov.tr/en/dataset/istanbul-barajlari-gunluk-doluluk-oranlari/resource/af0b3902-cfd9-4096-85f7-e2c3017e4f21. data span period late October 2000 mid-February 2024.

title: All Istanbul Dams Data — AllDams
code: AllDams

title: All Istanbul Dams Data — AllDams
text: data frame 8520 rows 13 variables.

title: Two-dimensional Clayton Copula — Clayton_Copula
text: joint cumulative distribution probability density functions two-dimensional Clayton copula.

title: Two-dimensional Clayton Copula — Clayton_Copula
code: Clayton_Copula(u, v, theta)  Clayton_Copula_pdf(u, v, theta)

title: Two-dimensional Clayton Copula — Clayton_Copula
text: u vector points \([0,1]\) representing first coordinate copula. v vector points \([0,1]\) representing first coordinate copula. theta defines \(\theta\) dependency parameter pairs.

title: Two-dimensional Clayton Copula — Clayton_Copula
text: Clayton_Copula gives joint cumulative distribution function, Clayton_Copula_pdf gives joint probability density function values Clayton copula evaluated points \((u,v)\) given \(\theta\) parameter.

title: Two-dimensional Clayton Copula — Clayton_Copula
text: joint distribution function two-dimensional Clayton copula, along joint probability density function, given $$C(u,v;\theta) = \left(u^{-\theta} + v^{-\theta} - 1\right)^{-1/\theta}, \; \theta \(0, \infty).$$ $$c_{\theta}(u,v) = (\theta +1)  u^{-(\theta + 1)} v^{-(\theta + 1)} \left( u^{-\theta} + v^{-\theta}-1 \right)^{-\left (\frac{1}{\theta} + 2 \right)}$$ \(\theta > 0\).

what: One-step LSE estimate of the Clayton copula parameter — LSE_clayton_onestep
title: One-step LSE estimate of the Clayton copula parameter — LSE_clayton_onestep

title: The Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) — ModifiedWeibull
text: probability density function, cumulative distribution function, quantile function, random generation hazard function Modified Weibull distribution (MWD) Lai et al. (2003).

title: The Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) — ModifiedWeibull
code: dMweibull(x, a, b, lambda, log = FALSE)  pMweibull(q, a, b, lambda, lower.tail = TRUE, log = FALSE)  qMweibull(p, a, b, lambda, lower.tail = TRUE)  rMweibull(n, a, b, lambda)  hMweibull(x, a, b, lambda, log = FALSE)

title: The Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) — ModifiedWeibull
text: x Numeric vector observations. Positive scale parameter distribution (\(> 0\)). b Non-negative shape parameter distribution (\(b \ge 0\)). lambda Non-negative acceleration flexibility parameter (\(\lambda \ge 0\)) governs quickly risk failure (hazard) grows time. Larger values correspond faster failure accumulation higher fragility, restricting \(\lambda \ge 0\) ensures non-decreasing hazard function. log Logical; TRUE, \(log(f(t))\) \(log(F(t))\) \(log(h(t))\) returned. q Numeric vector quantiles. lower.tail Logical; FALSE, \(1-F(t)\) returned quantiles computed \(1-p\). p Numeric vector probabilities \([0,1]\). n Integer; number observations generated.

title: The Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) — ModifiedWeibull
text: dMweibull gives probability density function, pMweibull gives cumulative distribution function, qMweibull gives quantile function, rMweibull generates random deviates, hMweibull gives hazard function.

title: The Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) — ModifiedWeibull
text: Modified Weibull distribution parameters \(\), \(b\) \(\lambda\) cumulative distribution density hazard functions given $$F(x) = 1 - \exp(-x^b \exp(\lambda x)),$$ $$f(x) = (b+ \lambda x) x^{b - 1} \exp(\lambda x) \exp(-x^b \exp(\lambda x)),$$ $$h(x) = (b+ \lambda x) x^{b - 1} \exp(\lambda x), $$ \(x > 0\), \(> 0\) scale parameter, \(b \ge 0\) shape parameter, \(\lambda \ge 0\) acceleration flexibility parameter controls quickly hazard grows time. \(\lambda = 0\), MWD reduces two-parameter Weibull distribution \(F(x) = 1 - \exp(-x^b)\) scale parameter \(\) shape parameter \(b\). \(b = 0\), MWD reduces type extreme-value (log-gamma) distribution \(F(x) = 1 - \exp(-\exp(\lambda x)) \).

title: The Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) — ModifiedWeibull
text: Lai, C. D., Xie, M., Murthy, D. N. P. (2003). https://doi.org/10.1109/TR.2002.805788 IEEE Transactions Reliability, 52(1), 33–37.

title: The Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) — ModifiedWeibull
code: n <- 25 a <- 0.75; b <- 1.25; lambda <- 0.60 set.seed(123) x <- rlnorm(n) ff <- dMweibull(x, a, b, lambda) FF <- pMweibull(x, a, b, lambda) qq <- qMweibull(runif(n), a, b, lambda) dat <- rMweibull(n, a, b, lambda) hf <- hMweibull(x, a, b, lambda )

what: Stress-Strength Reliability of MWD under Clayton copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD
title: Stress-Strength Reliability of MWD under Clayton copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD
text: main function evaluate stress-strength reliability (SSR) \(R=P(X>Y)\) \(X \sim MWD(a_1,b_1,\lambda_1)\) strength \(Y \sim MWD(a_2,b_2,\lambda_2)\) stress variables follow MWD dependency constructed Clayton copula.

title: Stress-Strength Reliability of MWD under Clayton copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD
code: Reliability_Clayton_MWD(a1, b1, lambda1, a2, b2, lambda2, theta)

title: Stress-Strength Reliability of MWD under Clayton copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD
text: a1, b1, lambda1 MWD parameters strength variable \(X\), \(a_1 > 0\), \(b_1 \ge 0\), \(\lambda_1 \ge 0\). a2, b2, lambda2 MWD parameters stress variable \(Y\), \(a_2 > 0\), \(b_2 \ge 0\), \(\lambda_2 \ge 0\). theta Clayton copula parameter \(\theta >0\).

title: Stress-Strength Reliability of MWD under Clayton copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD
text: value integrate function stats. value final estimate integral. abs.error Estimate modulus absolute error.

title: Stress-Strength Reliability of MWD under Clayton copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD
text: SSR \(R\) given $$ R = \int_{0}^{\infty} F_X(x)^{-(\theta + 1)} \left( F_X(x)^{-\theta} + G_Y(x)^{-\theta} - 1 \right)^{-\left(\frac{1}{\theta}+1\right)} f_X(x)\, dx, $$ can also written $$ R = \int_{0}^{1} t^{-(\theta + 1)} \left( t^{-\theta} + G_Y(F_X^{-1}(t))^{-\theta} - 1 \right)^{-\left(\frac{1}{\theta}+1\right)} dt, $$ \(F_X(x) = 1 - \exp(-a_1 x^{b_1} e^{\lambda_1 x})\) \(G_Y(y) = 1 - \exp(-a_2 y^{b_2} e^{\lambda_2 y})\). details can found Kızılaslan (2026).

title: Stress-Strength Reliability of MWD under Clayton copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD
text: Kızılaslan, F. (2026). Reliability estimation dependent stress–strength model Clayton copula modified Weibull margins. arXiv:????

title: Stress-Strength Reliability of MWD under Clayton copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD
code: a1 <- 0.75; b1 <- 1.5; lambda1 <- 0.6 a2 <- 1.2; b2 <- 0.5; lambda2 <- 0.9 theta <- 1 # 2, 3, 4, 5  R = Reliability_Clayton_MWD(a1, b1, lambda1, a2, b2, lambda2, theta) R$value #> [1] 0.8805338  R_MC = Reliability_Clayton_MWD_MC(a1, b1, lambda1, a2, b2, lambda2, theta, N = 100000) R_MC$R #> [1] 0.8802808

title: Monte Carlo Estimation of Stress-Strength Reliability for MWD under Clayton Copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD_MC
code: Reliability_Clayton_MWD_MC(a1, b1, lambda1, a2, b2, lambda2, theta, N = 10000)

title: Monte Carlo Estimation of Stress-Strength Reliability for MWD under Clayton Copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD_MC
text: a1, b1, lambda1 MWD parameters strength variable \(X\), \(a_1 > 0\), \(b_1 \ge 0\), \(\lambda_1 \ge 0\). a2, b2, lambda2 MWD parameters stress variable \(Y\), \(a_2 > 0\), \(b_2 \ge 0\), \(\lambda_2 \ge 0\). theta Clayton copula parameter \(\theta >0\). N Generated sample size \(X \sim MWD(a_1,b_1,\lambda_1)\) Monte Carlo Integration method.

title: Monte Carlo Estimation of Stress-Strength Reliability for MWD under Clayton Copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD_MC
text: list containing: R Approximated reliability value based \(N\) generated random samples. R_MCsample Monte Carlo approximate reliability \(R\) based \(N\) generated samples. sample Generated random sample size \(N\) \(MWD(a_1, b_1, \lambda_1)\).

title: Monte Carlo Estimation of Stress-Strength Reliability for MWD under Clayton Copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD_MC
text: approximate SSR \(R\) computed using Monte Carlo integration: $$ R \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{=1}^{N} T(x_i; \bm{\Omega_1}, \bm{\Omega_2}, \theta) = \tilde{R}, $$ \(\bm{\Omega_1} = (a_1, b_1, \lambda_1)\) \(\bm{\Omega_2} = (a_2, b_2, \lambda_2)\) denote marginal parameter vectors. function $$ T(x; \bm{\Omega_1}, \bm{\Omega_2}, \theta) = F_X(x)^{-(\theta + 1)} \left( F_X(x)^{-\theta} + G_Y(x)^{-\theta} - 1 \right)^{-\left(\frac{1}{\theta}+1\right)}, $$ \(x_1, \dots, x_N\) random sample generated Modified Weibull distribution \(MWD(a_1, b_1, \lambda_1)\). accuracy approximation improves increasing sample size \(N\). practice, values around \(N = 10^5\) provide accurate numerical results. details can found Kızılaslan (2026).

title: Monte Carlo Estimation of Stress-Strength Reliability for MWD under Clayton Copula — Reliability_Clayton_MWD_MC
text: Kızılaslan, F. (2026). Reliability estimation dependent stress–strength model Clayton copula modified Weibull margins. arXiv:????

what: Estimating parameters of SSR Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula — fit.SSR.ClaytonMWD
title: Estimating parameters of SSR Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula — fit.SSR.ClaytonMWD
text: Estimates parameters model parameters \((a_1, b_1,\lambda_1)\) strength variable \(X\), \((a_2, b_2, \lambda_2)\) stress variable \(Y\), \(\theta\) Clayton copula parameter. Fits dependent stress–strength reliability (SSR) model stress strength marginal distributions follow Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD), dependence modeled using Clayton copula. function estimates marginal copula parameters using several classical methods, including Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), Least Squares Estimation (LSE), Weighted Least Squares Estimation (WLSE), Maximum Product Spacings (MPS).

title: Estimating parameters of SSR Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula — fit.SSR.ClaytonMWD

title: Estimating parameters of SSR Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula — fit.SSR.ClaytonMWD
text: data list two numeric vectors: \(X\) (strength) \(Y\) (stress). ACI Logical; TRUE, \(95\%\) asymptotic confidence intervals based MLEs computed. bootstrap Logical; TRUE, \(95\%\) parametric bootstrap intervals computed. B Number bootstrap samples. seed Integer seed reproducibility. one.step Logical; TRUE, one-step LSE WLSE methods used estimating \(\theta\). alpha Numeric; significance level confidence intervals (e.g., \(0.05\) \(95\%\) CI). verbose Logical; TRUE, progress intermediate results optimization procedure printed. Default FALSE.

title: Estimating parameters of SSR Model with MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula — fit.SSR.ClaytonMWD

ACI.parameters ACI=TRUE, represents lower upper bounds \(100(1-\alpha)%\) ACI parameters length. boot.mle bootstrap=TRUE, represents lower upper bounds \(100(1-\alpha)%\) parametric bootstrap intervals parameters based MLE. boot.lse bootstrap=TRUE, represents lower upper bounds \(100(1-\alpha)%\) parametric bootstrap intervals parameters based LSE. boot.wlse bootstrap=TRUE, represents lower upper bounds \(100(1-\alpha)%\) parametric bootstrap intervals parameters based WLSE. boot.mps bootstrap=TRUE, represents lower upper bounds \(100(1-\alpha)%\) parametric bootstrap intervals parameters based MPS. boot.samples list containing bootstrap samples acrross parameters methods.

title: Estimating parameter of the Clayton copula — fitClayton
code: fitClayton(   x,   y,   est.method,   opt.method,   start,   estimates,   lower = NULL,   upper = NULL,   verbose = FALSE,   ... )

title: Estimating parameter of the Clayton copula — fitClayton
text: x Vector observations strength variable \(X\). y Vector observations stress variable \(Y\). est.method Used method estimating parameters maximum likelihood estimate "MLE", least square estimation "LSE", weighted least square estimation "WLSE", maximum product spacing estimates "MPS". opt.method optimization method optim function "Nelder-Mead", "BFGS", "CG", "L-BFGS-B", "SANN" "Brent" used estimating parameters. start Initial value \(\theta\) parameter optimized . estimates list containing estimates model paramters \(a_1,b_1,\lambda_1, a_2, b_2, \lambda_2\). lower Numeric vector specifying lower bounds parameters bounded optimization methods (e.g., "L-BFGS-B"). Ignored NULL. upper Numeric vector specifying upper bounds parameters bounded optimization methods. Ignored NULL. verbose Logical; TRUE, progress intermediate results optimization procedure printed. Default FALSE. ... arguments passed optim function lower upper limits, hessian, etc.

title: Estimating parameter of the Clayton copula — fitClayton
text: list containing: estimate Estimate Clayton copula parameter, \(\theta\). opt.fit Full optimization result.

title: Estimating parameter of the Clayton copula — fitClayton
text: joint distribution function two-dimensional Clayton copula $$ C(u,v;\theta) = \left(u^{-\theta} + v^{-\theta} - 1\right)^{-1/\theta}, $$ dependence parameter satisfies \(\theta > 0\). estimated using several classical methods: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE): Obtains parameter estimates maximizing log-likelihood function observed data assumed MWD distribution. Least Squares Estimation (LSE): Estimates parameters minimizing sum squared differences empirical distribution function theoretical distribution function MWD. Benard’s approximation used empirical distribution function ordered observations \(F(x_()) = (-0.3)/(n+0.4), =1,...,n.\). Weighted Least Squares Estimation (WLSE): modification LSE assigns weights squared differences. weights taken \(w_i = ((n+1)^2(n+2)) / ((n-+1)), =1,...,n. \) Maximum Product Spacings (MPS): Estimates parameters maximizing product spacings consecutive values fitted distribution function, providing robust alternative MLE, particularly small samples. details can found Kızılaslan (2026).

title: Estimating parameter of the Clayton copula — fitClayton
text: Kızılaslan, F. (2026). Reliability estimation dependent stress–strength model Clayton copula modified Weibull margins. arXiv preprint. Available https://arxiv.org/abs/????.

title: Estimating parameters of the Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) — fitMWD

title: Estimating parameters of the Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) — fitMWD
text: Classical estimations parameters Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) Modified Weibull Distribution (Lai et al., 2003) cumulative distribution function (CDF) probability density function (PDF) given $$ F(x) = 1 - \exp\left(-x^b \exp(\lambda x)\right), $$ $$ f(x) = (b + \lambda x) x^{b - 1} \exp(\lambda x) \exp\left(-x^b \exp(\lambda x)\right), $$ \(x > 0\), \(> 0\) scale parameter, \(b \ge 0\) shape parameter, \(\lambda \ge 0\) flexibility parameter controlling growth rate hazard function. model parameters estimated using several classical methods: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE): Obtains parameter estimates maximizing log-likelihood function observed data assumed MWD distribution. Least Squares Estimation (LSE): Estimates parameters minimizing sum squared differences empirical distribution function theoretical distribution function MWD. Benard’s approximation used empirical distribution function ordered observations \(F(x_()) = (-0.3)/(n+0.4), =1,...,n.\). Weighted Least Squares Estimation (WLSE): modification LSE assigns weights squared differences. weights taken \(w_i = ((n+1)^2(n+2)) / ((n-+1)), =1,...,n. \) Maximum Product Spacings (MPS): Estimates parameters maximizing product spacings consecutive values fitted distribution function, providing robust alternative MLE, particularly small samples. details can found Kızılaslan (2026).

title: Estimating parameters of the Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) — fitMWD
text: Lai, C. D., Xie, M., Murthy, D. N. P. (2003). https://doi.org/10.1109/TR.2002.805788 IEEE Transactions Reliability, 52(1), 33–37. Kızılaslan, F. (2026). Reliability estimation dependent stress–strength model Clayton copula modified Weibull margins. arXiv preprint. Available https://arxiv.org/abs/????.

title: Estimating parameters of the Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD) — fitMWD
code: # generate data from MWD(a, b, lambda) n <- 100 a <- 0.75; b <- 1.25; lambda <- 0.60 set.seed(123) dat <- rMweibull(n, a, b, lambda) init <- runif(3)  # Fit MWD to dat. fit.mle <- fitMWD(data = dat, est.method = "mle", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,                   lower = c(1e-05,1e-05,1e-05), upper = c(Inf,Inf,Inf), hessian = FALSE ) fit.mle$estimates #>         a         b    lambda  #> 0.7231634 1.2600843 0.6559157   fit.lse <- fitMWD(data = dat, est.method = "lse", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,                   lower = c(1e-05,1e-05,1e-05), upper = c(Inf,Inf,Inf), hessian = FALSE ) fit.lse$estimates #>         a         b    lambda  #> 0.9299033 1.4069386 0.4020883   fit.wlse <- fitMWD(data = dat, est.method = "wlse", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,                    lower = c(1e-05,1e-05,1e-05), upper = c(Inf,Inf,Inf), hessian = FALSE ) fit.wlse$estimates #>         a         b    lambda  #> 0.9220048 1.4228131 0.4207337   fit.mps <- fitMWD(data = dat, est.method = "mps", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,                   lower = c(1e-05,1e-05,1e-05), upper = c(Inf,Inf,Inf), hessian = FALSE ) fit.mps$estimates #>        a        b   lambda  #> 0.714056 1.189107 0.646072

title: Estimating parameters of the two-parameter Weibull distribution — fitWD
text: Classical estimations fo parameters two-parameter Weibull distribution two-parameter Weibull Distribution cumulative distribution function (CDF) probability density function (PDF) given $$F(x) = 1 - \exp(-x^b),$$ $$f(x) = b x^{b - 1} \exp(-x^b ),$$ \(x > 0\), \(> 0\) scale parameter \(b > 0\) shape parameter. model parameters estimated using several classical methods: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE): Obtains parameter estimates maximizing log-likelihood function observed data assumed two-parameter Weibull distribution. Least Squares Estimation (LSE): Estimates parameters minimizing sum squared differences empirical distribution function theoretical distribution function MWD. Benard’s approximation used empirical distribution function ordered observations \(F(x_()) = (-0.3)/(n+0.4), =1,...,n.\). Weighted Least Squares Estimation (WLSE): modification LSE assigns weights squared differences. weights taken \(w_i = ((n+1)^2(n+2)) / ((n-+1)), =1,...,n. \) Maximum Product Spacings (MPS): Estimates parameters maximizing product spacings consecutive values fitted distribution function, providing robust alternative MLE, particularly small samples.

title: Estimating parameters of the two-parameter Weibull distribution — fitWD
code: # generate data from WD(a, b) n <- 50 a <- 0.75; b <- 1.25; lambda <- 0 # reduces two-parameter Weibull distribution set.seed(123) X <- rMweibull(n, a, b, lambda) init <- runif(2)  # fit model fit.mle <- fitWD(data = X, est.method = "mle", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,                  lower = c(1e-05,1e-05), upper = c(Inf,Inf), hessian = FALSE ) fit.mle$estimates #>        a        b  #> 0.675279 1.283785   fit.lse <- fitWD(data = X, est.method = "lse", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,                  lower = c(1e-05,1e-05), upper = c(Inf,Inf), hessian = FALSE ) fit.lse$estimates #>         a         b  #> 0.7012337 1.2147202   fit.wlse <- fitWD(data = X, est.method = "wlse", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,                   lower = c(1e-05,1e-05), upper = c(Inf,Inf), hessian = FALSE ) fit.wlse$estimates #>        a        b  #> 0.690969 1.236661   fit.mps <- fitWD(data = X, est.method = "mps", opt.method = "L-BFGS-B", starts = init,                  lower = c(1e-05,1e-05), upper = c(Inf,Inf), hessian = FALSE ) fit.mps$estimates #>         a         b  #> 0.6848478 1.1977846

title: Parametric Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — parametric_bootstrap
text: gives parametric bootstrap confidence interval estimation unknown parameters along reliability \(R\) based MLE, LSE, WLSE MPS methods.

title: Parametric Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — parametric_bootstrap
code: parametric_bootstrap(   est.method,   opt.method,   boot.estimates,   n,   B = 1000,   seed = NULL,   one.step = TRUE,   alpha = 0.05 )

title: Parametric Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — parametric_bootstrap
text: est.method Used method estimating parameters maximum likelihood estimate "MLE", least square estimation "LSE", weighted least square estimation "WLSE", maximum product spacing estimates "MPS". opt.method optimization method optim function "Nelder-Mead", "BFGS", "CG", "L-BFGS-B", "SANN" "Brent" used estimating parameters. boot.estimates list containing estimates unknown parameters. elements \((a_1, b_1, \lambda_1)\) correspond strength variable, \((a_2, b_2, \lambda_2)\) correspond stress variable, \(\theta\) corresponds Clayton copula parameter. n sample size B Number bootstrap samples. seed Integer seed reproducibility. one.step Logical; TRUE, one-step LSE WLSE methods used estimating \(\theta\). alpha Numeric; significance level confidence intervals (e.g., \(0.05\) \(95\%\) CI).

title: Parametric Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — parametric_bootstrap
text: list containing: parameters.quantiles gives \(100(1-\alpha)\%\) lower upper bounds. boot.results represents estimates values parameters B bootstrap samples.

title: Parametric Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — parametric_bootstrap
text: parametric bootstrap percentile method employed able construct bootstrap confidence intervals unknown parameters \(R\) well based MLE, LSE, WLSE MPSE methods. details can found Kızılaslan (2026).

title: Parametric Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — parametric_bootstrap

what: Bivariate Random Data Generation for MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula — rMweibull_Clayton
title: Bivariate Random Data Generation for MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula — rMweibull_Clayton
text: Generates bivariate random samples dependent stress–strength model marginals follow Modified Weibull Distribution (MWD), dependence structure variables modeled using Clayton copula.

title: Bivariate Random Data Generation for MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula — rMweibull_Clayton
code: rMweibull_Clayton(n, a1, b1, lambda1, a2, b2, lambda2, theta)

title: Bivariate Random Data Generation for MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula — rMweibull_Clayton
text: n Integer; number observations generated. a1, b1, lambda1 MWD parameters strength variable \(X\), \(a_1 > 0\), \(b_1 \ge 0\), \(\lambda_1 \ge 0\). a2, b2, lambda2 MWD parameters stress variable \(Y\), \(a_2 > 0\), \(b_2 \ge 0\), \(\lambda_2 \ge 0\). theta Clayton copula parameter \(\theta >0\).

title: Bivariate Random Data Generation for MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula — rMweibull_Clayton
text: list containing: (U, V) Independent generated uniform numbers \((X,Y)\). (X, Y) Dependent generated \(n\) pairs \((X,Y)\) observations.

title: Bivariate Random Data Generation for MWD Marginals via Clayton Copula — rMweibull_Clayton
text: details provided Kızılaslan (2026).

title: Kendall's Tau Estimate of Clayton copula parameter — theta_Ktau_estimate
text: Moment estimate \(\theta\) parameter based Kendall's \(\tau\).

title: Kendall's Tau Estimate of Clayton copula parameter — theta_Ktau_estimate
code: set.seed(123) n <- 50 a1 <- 0.75; b1 <- 1.5; lambda1 <- 0.6 a2 <- 1.2; b2 <- 0.5; lambda2 <- 0.9 theta <- 5 # 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 # data generation dat <- SSReliabilityClaytonMWD::rMweibull_Clayton(n, a1, b1, lambda1, a2, b2, lambda2, theta) theta_Ktau_estimate(dat) #> [1] 4.920904
